architecture_summary: The paper studies scaling laws for Vision Transformers, refining ViT training and architecture with decoupled head/body weight decay, removing the [class] token in favor of GAP/MAP pooling, using memory-efficient optimizers, and a reciprocal square-root learning-rate schedule. It scales ViT models and data up to ViT-G/14 with nearly two billion parameters and reports improved transfer and state-of-the-art ImageNet performance.

Figure 1. Few-shot transfer results. Our ViT-G model reaches 84.86% top-1 accuracy on ImageNet with 10-shot linear evaluation.
In addition, with just a single example per class on the ImageNet dataset (which has 1 000 classes), our best model achieves 69.52% accuracy; and with 10 examples per class it attains 84.86%.

novel_contributions: We scale ViT models and data, both up and down, and characterize the relationships between error rate, data, and compute.
We refine the architecture and training of ViT, reducing memory consumption and increasing accuracy of the resulting models.
We discover that very strong L2 regularization, applied to the final linear prediction layer only, results in a learned visual representation that has very strong few-shot transfer capabilities.
We substantially reduce the memory footprint of the original ViT model proposed in [16].
We train a model with two billion parameters and attain a new state-of-the-art 90.45% accuracy on ImageNet.

key_claims.claim: The paper characterizes scaling laws for Vision Transformers across model size, dataset size, and compute, and finds a saturating power-law frontier.
Scaling up compute, model, and data together improves representation quality, but small models and small datasets saturate and fall off the frontier.
key_claims.evidence_section: 2.1. Scaling up compute, model and data together

key_claims.claim: Bigger models are more sample efficient, needing fewer seen images to reach the same error rate.
key_claims.evidence_section: 2.3. Big models are more sample efficient

key_claims.claim: The improved training recipe yields very strong few-shot transfer, including 69.52% with one example per class and 84.86% with 10 examples per class on ImageNet.
ViT-G/14 achieves 90.45% top-1 accuracy on ImageNet and is presented as a new state-of-the-art result.
The paper reduces ViT memory usage by removing the [class] token and using GAP/MAP pooling, with MAP selected as the final head.
The paper introduces memory-efficient optimizer modifications, including half-precision momentum and a modified AdaFactor, enabling training of very large ViT models.
